tracks.description: The first part of this piece, titled ''Sunrise,'' became famous after being used in the film 2001: A Space Odyssey.
Later given new lyrics and adopted as the National Anthem of the United States, this song was originally written in 1780 for the London's Anacreontic Society.
An extremely dark, edgy, and cool electronic track. Are U Ready... to take on these clutch beats?
This Korean folk song is over 600 years old and has more than 3,600 variations. The lyrics tell of a love story while evoking longing and sentimentality.
Classic folk song commonly sung on New Years, with lyrics from a Scottish poem and music based on a traditional Scots folk melody.
Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons! Baboons!''

tracks.trackname_long: Take Me Out To The Ball Game
tracks.description: This has become the theme song of North American baseball, although neither of the song's authors had attended a game at the time of its writing.
Based on a French comedy of the same name, this famous opera may have been composed in only three weeks! For comparison, we've been working on this game for three hundred weeks!
One of the most famous classical pieces of all time. Beethoven really killed it with this one.
The most recognized song in the English language. We've all sung this one before... but now you can play it on the trombone!
This electronic track is an attempt to capture what it feels like to play the trombone.
Originally considered only a mild success, this Austrian waltz has become of the most popular pieces of music in the classical repertoire.
This widely-known waltz is often played by new students of the piano, and now you can finally play it on a disgusting virtual trombone!
Commander Tokyo is taking the world by storm with his innovative dance moves! Can you keep up?
This famous song became popularized among railroad workers in the late 19th century, but has much older roots as a spiritual.
This African American spiritual, which dates back to before the Civil War, is now a staple of New Orleans Jazz.
This serenade was written for strings, but we've added trombone and ''trap drums.'' Mozart would have approved.
The Trombone Champ played this so badly, while reeking of hot dogs so horrendously, that Music as we know it was destroyed and reborn.
This piece, performed solo or sold as rolls for automated player pianos, sounds much better with the addition of a trombone.
One of the fastest and most chaotic well-known classical pieces, this legendary interlude is guaranteed to test your skills!

Under a hard season, fired up by the sun languishes the Tromboner. Soft breezes stir the air, but threatening the North Wind sweeps them suddenly aside. The Tromboner trembles, fearing violent storms and his fate...
This famous Italian song was written to commemorate the opening of a funicular railway on Mount Vesuvius. But nowadays, it makes people think of pizza pizza pizza!
Named because of Fucik's personal interest in the Roman Empire, this has become one of the most widely-known pieces of circus music.
You'll need a "rest"... after attempting this fast jazz rendition of the timeless, traditional English Christmas Carol!
The royal anthem in most Commonwealth realms and their territories, this is also known as ''My Country Tis Of Thee'' in the United States.
One of the most famous solo piano pieces ever written, Gymnopédie No.1 is also considered an important precursor to modern ambient music.
This famous French aria, from the opera ''Carmen,'' may have had its melody based on a Spanish dance number. Everyone agrees: it sounds much better with a trombone instead of all that singing.

tracks.trackname_long: In The Hall of the Mountain King
tracks.description: This famous piece, written for the play ''Peer Gynt,'' accompanies a scene in which the main character meets a host of trolls and goblins. Very spooky!
One of the first modern folk songs in the Hebrew language, this has become a staple at Jewish weddings and bar/bat mitzvah celebrations.
Did you know? This may be the first popular song to mention a telephone. The telephone had only been recently invented when it was written! For comparison, the trombone is around 600 years old.
The rousing The National Anthem of Brazil, which was formally given lyrics on the Independence Centennial of 1922. This rendition was performed by the US Navy Band.

tracks.trackname_long: The House of the Rising Sun
tracks.description: This famous blues song, telling of a person's life gone wrong in New Orleans, may have roots in a traditional English folk song.
Commonly performed by mariachi bands, this is the national dance of Mexico. The dance represents a courtship ritual and absolutely slaps.
Arguably the most famous Christmas song of all time! Celebrate the holidays with this jazzy rendition.
Martians attack in this remaster of a track from an old Holy Wow album, titled ''Choose Your Own Adventure!''

You'll probably recognize this famous Russian folk song from a famous puzzle game! The original song tells the story of the meeting between a peddler and a woman... This version tells no story at all, because the vocals have been replaced by trombone honking sounds.
Adopted as France's national anthem in 1795, this rousing European march is also used as a song of revolution. This rendition was performed by the US Navy Band.
Written by the seven-time Mayor of Baltimore, Maryland, some believe this song is about the horse ''Lady Suffolk.''
The most famous piece from Holst's orchestral suite ''The Planets,'' this is also one of the most famous pieces written in a 5/4 time signature.
Our sad attempt at composing a merengue song, a fast paced Latin American dance originating in the Dominican Republic.
One of the most popular songs in China, this piece dates back to the Qing dynasty and has several sets of lyrics - although all center around the titular Jasmine flower.
Inspired by Russian literature and evoking images of witchcraft rituals performed in the dark of night, this famous piece was never performed during Mussorgsky's lifetime.
Originally commissioned for the Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day banquet in 1880, this didn't become the de facto anthem until 1939.
This timeless Irish ballad, set to the tune of ''Londonderry Air,'' isn't typically played at full volume on a trombone. Time to change that!
Loosely based on a ode by Friedrich Schiller, this heavenly piece was adopted as the ''Anthem of Europe'' in 1972.
This famous folk song about a dude with a farm dates back to the 1700s, or possibly earlier. His animals make funny noises, and now you can too!
Originally a German folk song about a fir tree, this later became one of the most famous Christmas songs of all time!
This polka, originally written by a Czech musician, swept the world in popularity throughout World War II.
This jaunty little jig, remixed by MAX TUNDRA, has arrived to put your skills to the test! Prepare yourself for two minutes of seafaring brutality!

tracks.trackname_long: When the Saints Go Marching In
tracks.description: This famous song, commonly associated with New Orleans Jazz, may have evolved from early gospel pieces.
This traditional Japanese folk song, dating back to the Edo period, celebrates the beautiful Cherry Blossoms of spring.
The titular ''silent'' night is not so silent in this club mix by legendary producer Max Tundra!
A ska song, because everyone online seemed to want one. Are you happy now? Please leave me alone.
Possibly derived from a Creole folk song, this dance was popular during Abraham Lincoln's youth in southern Indiana.
Alas, Trombone Skyze is gloriously reborn! Do you have what it takes to honk along with this fast, breakbeat remix?
After a 1987 act of the U.S. Congress, this became the official National March of the United States of America.
In this parody of a classic jazz standard, a musician sees his trombone at a trombonery and contemplates his life as a tromboner.

tracks.trackname_long: Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy
tracks.description: This piece, from the second act of The Nutcracker ballet, is instantly recognizable and has earned enormous popularity.
This twenty-four-note military melody, commonly played at military funerals, was originally used to signal lights out. It's very important that you play this respectfully!
Congrats on beating the game! Wow... You did it! As a reward, we hope you enjoy this medley of the various songs you encountered throughout this game!
This famous Bach piece for organ has mysterious origins and was not widely known until the 1800s! Finally, you can spruce up this spooky tune with trombone toots!
An original electronic piece written by the legendary maximalist MAX TUNDRA.
Warm up your trombone with this simple warm-up. Perfect for first-timers!
Originally the overture for an opera, this piece has been widely used, and widely parodied, over time.
This Sousa piece was written for an essay contest and became one of the most popular marches of all time. This rendition was performed by the US Marine Band.
